---
title: "Logistic Regression"
---

<span class="section-badge advanced">Advanced Topics</span>

Use logistic regression when your **outcome variable is categorical** — most commonly binary (yes/no, clinical/control, recovered/not recovered). Linear regression is inappropriate for binary outcomes because it can produce predicted probabilities below 0 or above 1, and it violates homoscedasticity.

---

## What is it?

Binary logistic regression models the **log-odds** (logit) of the outcome as a linear function of the predictors:

$$\log\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = b_0 + b_1 X_1 + b_2 X_2 + \ldots$$

The model predicts the **probability** that a case belongs to the "outcome = 1" category. Predictors can be continuous, binary, or categorical (with dummy coding).

### Odds ratios

The exponentiated coefficients (*e^b*) are **odds ratios (OR)**:

| OR | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| > 1 | A one-unit increase in X is associated with *increased* odds of the outcome |
| < 1 | A one-unit increase in X is associated with *decreased* odds of the outcome |
| = 1 | No association |

::: {.callout-note}
## Odds ≠ probability
An OR of 2 does not mean the outcome is "twice as likely" — it means the *odds* are doubled. Probabilities and odds are related but not the same. Always report predicted probabilities or absolute risk when communicating results to a non-statistical audience.
:::

---

## When to use logistic regression

| Situation | Appropriate method |
|---|---|
| Binary outcome (2 categories) | Binary logistic regression |
| Nominal outcome (3+ unordered categories) | Multinomial logistic regression |
| Ordinal outcome (3+ ordered categories) | Ordinal logistic regression |

This page focuses on **binary logistic regression** — the most common case in psychology research.

---

## Assumptions

::: {.assumption-list}
- **Binary outcome**: the DV must have exactly two categories
- **Independence of observations**: each participant contributes one row
- **No multicollinearity**: predictors should not be highly correlated — check VIF as in regular regression
- **Sufficient cell sizes**: especially important when predictors are categorical; very small cells can cause convergence problems
- **No complete separation**: if a predictor perfectly predicts the outcome, the model cannot estimate coefficients
- **Linearity of continuous predictors with the log-odds**: check using Box-Tidwell test or visual inspection (available as an option in JASP)
:::

*Note*: logistic regression does **not** require normality of residuals or homoscedasticity — these are linear regression assumptions that do not apply here.

---

## Model fit

Unlike linear regression, logistic regression uses maximum likelihood estimation. Model fit statistics:

| Statistic | What it tells you |
|---|---|
| **−2 Log Likelihood (−2LL)** | Overall fit — smaller is better |
| **Nagelkerke R²** | Pseudo-R²: approximate proportion of variance explained — treat as an effect size, not a literal R² |
| **Hosmer-Lemeshow test** | Goodness of fit — a non-significant result indicates adequate fit |
| **Classification table** | Percentage of cases correctly classified by the model |

Model comparison: test whether adding predictors improves fit using the **likelihood ratio test** (χ² difference in −2LL with corresponding df).

---

## How to do it in JASP

Go to **Regression → Logistic Regression**.

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## JASP Video Library — Logistic Regression
Recording at [jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#logistic-regression](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#logistic-regression):

- [▶ Logistic Regression](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#logistic-regression) — setup, odds ratios, classification table, model fit
:::

---

## Interpreting the output

**Omnibus test**: tests whether the model with predictors fits better than an intercept-only model. A significant χ² (*p* < .05) indicates the model explains a significant amount of variance.

**Coefficients table**: for each predictor, report:
- *b*: the log-odds coefficient (hard to interpret directly)
- *OR* (= *e^b*): the odds ratio — more interpretable
- 95% CI for the OR: does it include 1.0? If not, the predictor is significant
- *p*-value

::: {.callout-tip}
## APA reporting example
Logistic regression was used to predict survival from age and treatment group. The model was significant, χ²(2) = 14.3, *p* < .001, Nagelkerke R² = .23. Treatment group was a significant predictor, *b* = 1.42, OR = 4.14, 95% CI [1.87, 9.17], *p* = .001.
:::

---

## Multinomial logistic regression

When the outcome has **three or more unordered categories**, use **multinomial logistic regression**. This runs separate binary logistic regressions comparing each category to a reference category. Available in JASP under **Regression → Logistic Regression** by changing the outcome type.

---

## Further reading

- **DSUJ Chapter 17** — *Logistic regression* — binary and multinomial logistic regression with JASP walkthroughs

---

## Try it yourself

Each exercise below ends with a collapsible solution. Try the analysis first, then expand to compare.

### Exercise 1 (binary logistic regression — risk factors) — Which factors predict adolescent suicide risk?

Adolescents at risk of self-harm were measured on demographics, family environment, and **music preferences** (heavy metal among others). Using [`leni_17.jasp`](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_17.jasp), fit a binary logistic regression with risk status as the outcome. Report each predictor's odds ratio with its 95% CI and the Nagelkerke R².

::: {.callout-note collapse=true icon=false}
## ▸ Worked solution
- **Walkthrough**: [Labcoat Leni — Heavy metal and risk of harm](https://discoverjasp.com/pages/labcoat_leni#heavy-metal-and-risk-of-harm)
- **Annotated JASP output**: [leni_17.html](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_17.html)
:::

### Exercise 2 (binary logistic regression — developmental psychology) — Do age and theory-of-mind predict mastery of emotional display rules?

A psychologist measured whether each child could correctly apply a "display rule" (pass/fail), their age in months, and their theory-of-mind score. Using [`display.jasp`](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_data/data/display.jasp), fit a logistic regression with display-rule mastery as the binary outcome and age + theory-of-mind as predictors.

::: {.callout-note collapse=true icon=false}
## ▸ Worked solution
- **Walkthrough**: [Smart Alex Task 17.1](https://discoverjasp.com/pages/smart_alex#task-17.1)
- **Annotated JASP output**: [alex_17_01-02.html](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_alex/alex_17_01-02.html)
:::

### Exercise 3 (binary logistic regression — social psychology) — Does social class (inferred from car) predict whether a driver cuts in?

Piff et al. (2012) ranked drivers' social class by the type of car they drove (1–5) and observed whether each driver cut in front of another at a busy intersection. Using [`piff_2012_vehicle.jasp`](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_data/data/piff_2012_vehicle.jasp), fit a logistic regression with `vehicle_cut` (yes/no) as the outcome and `vehicle` (1–5) as the predictor. Interpret the odds ratio for a one-step increase in vehicle class.

::: {.callout-note collapse=true icon=false}
## ▸ Worked solution
- **Walkthrough**: [Smart Alex Task 17.3](https://discoverjasp.com/pages/smart_alex#task-17.3)
- **Annotated JASP output**: [alex_17_03.html](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_alex/alex_17_03.html)
:::
